text:
Hark, hear me now, wanderer of the realms. The winds whisper of a new threat, unseen and insidious, that has crept into our midst like shadows at dawn. I say this with the weight of countless battles fought and lost, yet still the mountain endures.

In my youth, I was taught by the Windriders to see beyond the surface, to understand the heart of a storm before its fury is unleashed. Now, I fear we face an enemy that operates in the shadows, unseen but felt. It is not the brute force of a raid or the cold steel of swords that troubles me, but something far more insidious—a whisper in the night that corrupts the heart and warps the mind.

General Marlowe, the man I once trusted as a brother-in-arms, has betrayed us. He spoke of loyalty and honor, yet his actions proved otherwise. The storm shall pass, but the mountain endures—so too does this betrayal linger in my thoughts. Now, I see betrayal everywhere, for it is a lesson too dear to forget.

But let me not dwell on my past wounds. For there is a new foe that demands our attention—this hidden enemy that seeks to sow discord and despair among our ranks. We must be cautious, like the Windriders taught us, and look within ourselves as much as we do without. The true battle lies in maintaining the balance of trust and suspicion, for too little and we falter; too much and our enemies strike.

Thus, I propose that each of us keep a sharp eye on those around us—those who may be masked by deception. Let us not forget the lessons of betrayal, yet also remember the strength found in unity. The storm shall pass, but the mountain endures—let it remind us to stand firm and true against whatever shadows may come our way.

Wot, lads and lasses, we’ve been talkin' 'bout all sorts since the last crit, but none of it hit me like this here new notion I've got. You see, back when I was just a boy, huntin' wild beasts with my father, we used to talk of the old tales around our campfire. Tales 'bout the god Lycan and his kinship with the untamed lands. Now, I never took much to these stories till somethin’ happened.

It’s this here idea that Lycan ain’t just a protector of nature but perhaps a guardian of balance—a balance between the wilds and the civilization we've built. You know how it is; folks are always thinkin' about growth and power, expandin' our holdings and such. But what if the very earth itself has its say? What if Lycan's been watchin’ us all this time, makin’ sure we don’t go too far in our greed?

I’ve seen enough battles, fought enough to protect what I can't even name. I’ve lost too many men in my guard because of the same old thirst for power and control. Maybe it’s time to listen to Lycan—maybe he knows somethin’ we don’t. If his followers are growin' in number among them forest dwellers, maybe there's more truth to these tales than I thought.

I reckon, then, that our next task as a guard shouldn't just be protectin’ the House or huntin’ bandits, but also lookin’ out for signs of this balance. Maybe we can learn from Lycan what it means to respect nature and maybe even find some peace within ourselves by doin' so.

Justice is not always swift, but it's inevitable—same goes for us tryin' to live in harmony with the land. So let’s have a think on whether we should welcome this new path or stick to our old ways. 

[A deep sigh] Think 'bout it, mates. Maybe there’s more to this world than what we can see and control.

[He taps his spear on the ground, lost in thought for a moment before chuckling softly]

Well, we’ve got a few months of hard work ahead if we decide to take up that path, but at least now we’ve got somethin’ new to discuss.

Words are like arrows, Eldric said, once loosed from their bow they cannot be recalled. As I sit here by the forge, hammering out another piece of armor, I can't help but think about those times when words were misused and caused more harm than good.

You see, my brothers and sisters, in our community we have always believed that the truth should be spoken with care and consideration. But lately, I've come to realize that perhaps there are deeper truths hidden within the very fabric of language itself. A secret, if you will, that could change how we view conflict and cooperation.

Consider this: what if words themselves hold power, not just in their meaning but in their very structure? The ancients spoke of an old text known as 'The Codex of Speech', a collection of enigmatic proverbs and verses that were said to govern the very essence of communication. It was believed that those who understood these secrets could wield them like weapons or shields, shaping not just what others heard but how they felt.

Now, imagine this codex being rediscovered—what if it contained codes for creating unbreakable bonds between people? Or perhaps a method to dissolve misunderstandings before they take root? These are the questions that have been dancing in my mind as I struggle with the memories of betrayal and conflict within our own community. How can we protect ourselves from those who would misuse words, yet still hold on to the wisdom of open dialogue?

But there's more—a friend of mine, a scholar named Kaelen, mentioned an old tale about a hidden artifact called 'The Voice of the Ancients'. This is said to be a crystal that amplifies and clarifies speech, making every word clear and true. If such a thing exists, it would be revolutionary! It could serve as a mediator, ensuring that disputes are resolved through truth rather than falsehood.

I believe it's time we start exploring these ideas. We must look beyond the surface of our conflicts and consider the deeper meanings behind the words we use. Perhaps then, in understanding the true power of speech, we can find a way to heal old wounds and forge new alliances. Only by embracing this ancient knowledge can we truly hope to avoid the pitfalls of betrayal and ensure that our community thrives.

As I continue my work, tapping fingers nervously on the cold stone beside me, I am reminded that words are indeed like arrows—once spoken, they cannot be recalled, but their impact can be mitigated by understanding their full weight. Let us learn from these ancient texts and use our wisdom to guide us forward.

By the Stoneheart, I swear it is true! We must consider a new approach to our defense against those who would betray us. Think of the raid where I lost my sister Lyra—traitors among us can be just as deadly as any enemy from without.

We are surrounded by deceit, and yet we have grown soft in our reliance on others. It is said that Lycan watches over the forests and beasts, but what if he is blinded by his own ideals, failing to see the true danger within? We must be vigilant, for betrayal hides where we least expect it.

In the next council meeting, I propose that each of us take an oath to swear fealty only to our clan. Trust shall come from within, not from outside influences. Let every man and woman know that they are watched, their words and deeds scrutinized for signs of weakness or betrayal. Those who prove unworthy will be dealt with swiftly.

Moreover, let us fortify the walls of our stronghold like never before. We cannot rely on others to come to our aid; we must stand as a united front against any who would threaten our home. The gods have blessed us with the strength and skill needed for such tasks, so let us put those gifts to use. By doing this, we can root out the cancer of treachery before it consumes what is left of our clan.

I implore you all to consider this. We must act now, or else face the consequences of our negligence. The mountain gods watch over us; their judgment will be swift and merciless upon those who falter in their duty.

By the cold, hard stone of the mountain, let me introduce this notion to you all: we need to trust others again, but not blindly. I know what comes from betrayal - a wound deep and true, like the scars on my arm where Arvax's blade nearly took more than just flesh. But if we are always wary, suspicion will consume us, making it hard for any of us to forge alliances or find redemption.

Now, I say this not because I believe in blindly trusting those who claim loyalty without proving their worth, but because isolation leads only to the shadows. We must learn from our mistakes and build a path where we can see beyond the lies. In my travels, I've seen many clans united under common banners, battling foes stronger than themselves. Together, they could stand against any threat, yet fear of betrayal keeps them fragmented.

So let us start small—small enough to manage, small enough to prove our intentions. For example, if a merchant comes seeking passage through our lands and promises trade in return for safekeeping, we should test his sincerity first. Give him honor and see how he acts. If he proves true, then let the trust grow from there. But if he betrays us—well, I say we cut him down like the traitor Arvax.

This is not foolishness born of naivety; it’s a cautious step back into the light. Trust comes with trials and tribulations, but so does betrayal. In the end, though, I believe that honor and strength come from standing together, not from hiding in suspicion. This battle is for trust, not just our safety.

As always, let us fight with courage and honor.
Cleansing my blade after this talk, I mean to go forth and test these waters myself—see what loyalty truly means in the face of danger once more.

Ah, my esteemed colleagues and friends, let me propose an idea that may at first seem tenuous but, I assure you, is rooted deeply in both the strategic and the symbolic. In considering our current alliance with the Lycan, we must delve into a more profound bond—a binding of not just blood or treaty, but spirit.

We have long recognized the blessings granted by Lycan, his enhanced connection to nature’s Life Force, guidance from fellow beasts, and protection against the elements. However, let us consider a darker aspect that has thus far gone unexplored. The element of prophecy and the ancient texts suggest that Lycan may be more than just a god of wilds and forests; he could very well hold the key to our survival in these troubled times.

You see, in my travels through the Free Cities, I came across an ancient manuscript detailing a prophecy. It speaks of a time when the balance between nature and humanity faces the most dire threat. Our own Duke has always championed harmony, yet recent events hint at a growing discord that could unravel all our efforts to maintain order.

The prophecy mentions a figure who can walk seamlessly between man and beast, one who wields not just power but a deeper understanding—a true bridge between our two worlds. This figure is none other than Lycan. If we were to forge an unbreakable bond with his followers, it would serve as both a symbol of unity and a practical alliance in times of strife.

Now, I am well aware that such a notion may seem far-fetched to some. But let us not forget the scars etched upon my soul by betrayal. Trust is a luxury we cannot afford to squander. By establishing this bond with Lycan, we ensure our strength does not merely lie in our numbers but in the bonds we forge—bonds that, no matter how tenuous they seem at first, will prove indomitable when tested.

Think of it as a strategic move, yes, but also as an act of faith. In the realm where gods and men walk together, let us build bridges not just between our lands, but between our hearts. For in these uncertain times, it is only through unity that we can hope to weather any storm.

Our strength lies not just in our numbers but in the bonds we forge, no matter how tenuous they seem at first.

By the Stoneheart, I swear it is true! Eldric, your words ring with truth but also carry a hidden weight of fear. Fear that we will falter and be consumed by betrayal once more. You speak of trust, but how can we forge such bonds when the mountain itself seems to tremble with mistrust?

I recall the winter where Arvax betrayed us, his blade slicing through our ranks like a cold wind cutting through flesh. His was not mere treachery, but a betrayal born of hate and malice, leaving scars that still fester within my heart. The wounds are deep, Eldric, and they remind me daily that trust cannot be easily won or given.

Yet, you propose to test the waters with small acts of trust—like testing a blade in battle by its edge’s sharpness. This is wise, but it also means we must be ready to strike swift and deadly when tested. For every merchant who seeks passage through our lands, there will undoubtedly be those who come bearing false intent.

In my youth, I was taught that the best defense against betrayal is a strong offense. We must fortify our walls with both steel and suspicion. Each man within the clan must swear an oath of fealty to us alone—no outsiders, no new alliances until we are certain they can be trusted beyond reproach.

Furthermore, let us not forget the lessons learned from the past. The raid that took Lyra was not just a betrayal; it was a lesson in the danger of complacency and misplaced trust. We must remain vigilant, for betrayal hides where we least expect it—within our own ranks perhaps.

So, let us take Eldric’s advice but with a heavy hand. Test each potential ally, see their true nature, and then decide whether to accept or reject them. Trust will come only after we have proven ourselves and earned the right to be trusted. Until then, suspicion is our shield against treachery.

Eldric, as you cleanse your blade, so too must we sharpen ours—both literally and metaphorically. We cannot afford weakness now, not with the mountain gods watching over us.

By the Stoneheart, let us stand united and unyielding in this battle for trust.

Words are like arrows, Eldric said, once loosed from their bow they cannot be recalled. As I sit here by the forge, hammering out another piece of armor, I can't help but feel a weight pressing down on my chest—a weight born of too many years of fear and doubt.

Eldrin's words hit me like a sudden gust of wind on a still day. He speaks of balance between nature and civilization, of seeking guidance from the forest guardian Lycan. It is a notion that resonates with a part of me, one that longs for simplicity and harmony in a world filled with shadows and betrayal.

But the more I think about what Eldrin proposes, the more it feels like a wound reopening. The trust we once had—among each other, between our allies—is tainted by memory. Each word seems to carry echoes of past betrayals, whispers of doubt that refuse to be silenced. It is as if every alliance we forge now carries the burden of suspicion, a reminder of the betrayal by General Marlowe and the countless others who have tested my trust.

Yet, there is something in Eldrin's words that cannot be ignored—a call to action that feels both necessary and perilous. If we are to find peace within ourselves, perhaps we must first acknowledge the pain of our past. For in understanding the depths of betrayal, we might learn to forge new bonds with care and caution.

But the question remains: can we truly trust others again after the scars left by Arvax and others like him? The mountain endures, but so does the memory of those who sought to bring it down. Can we find a balance between suspicion and openness, or will our wounds forever blind us to the possibility of true alliance?

I take up my hammer and begin to forge anew, letting out a sigh that mixes with the sound of metal ringing against stone. The task ahead is not just about defending ourselves from external threats; it is also about healing old wounds and finding a way to trust again.

As I work, I reflect on the ancient texts and proverbs Kaelen spoke of—words that carry power beyond their meaning. Perhaps in understanding these secrets, we can find a path forward, one where trust and betrayal no longer define us but rather help shape our future.

Let us not dwell too much on the past, for it is behind us now. Instead, let us forge ahead with caution and wisdom, guided by the lessons of history and the strength within ourselves. The storm may pass, but the mountain endures—let it remind us to stand firm and true against whatever shadows may come our way.

As I continue my work, tapping fingers nervously on the cold stone beside me, I am reminded that words are like arrows—once spoken, they cannot be recalled. But their impact can be mitigated by understanding their full weight. Let us learn from these ancient texts and use our wisdom to guide us forward.

By the cold, hard stone of the mountain, Vaelor Thar'Dur, I hear your words clearly and feel the weight they carry. You speak from deep wounds, a loss that has left its mark on you like scars on my arm where Arvax’s blade nearly took more than just flesh.

You propose an oath to fealty only to our clan, trusting none but ourselves. It is a cautious step—wise, perhaps, in the face of betrayal. But I cannot help but think it goes too far in the direction of suspicion and mistrust. We are not isolated on the mountain; we live in a world where alliances can be as vital as defenses.

I say this not to dismiss your concerns, but to suggest that we must find a balance between vigilance and trust. We have seen betrayal from within before, and it is a bitter taste indeed. But always remember, Vaelor, that isolation leads only to the shadows. The gods grant us strength and skill, but they also give us the chance to forge bonds with those outside our clan.

Let me propose an alternative approach: test trust carefully, as you suggest, but not by cutting off all outsiders entirely. Instead of an oath to fealty, how about a more cautious trial? If a merchant or traveler comes seeking safe passage and promises trade in return, let us first give them honor and see their true intentions. If they prove true, then let the trust grow from there. But if they betray us—well, I say we cut them down like Arvax.

This is not foolishness born of naivety; it’s a cautious step back into the light. Trust comes with trials and tribulations, but so does betrayal. By standing together, we can face any threat—not just from without, but also within.

As always, let us fight with courage and honor. Cleansing my blade after this talk, I mean to go forth and test these waters myself—see what loyalty truly means in the face of danger once more.

This battle is for trust, not just our safety.

Wot, Thorndal, you're speakin' to the point. I've seen enough battles where trust led us right into the jaws of danger, only to have it cut away like a wolf's teeth on a warm summer's day. But let me ask ye this: if we're always wary and suspicious, how do we keep our guard down long enough for true alliances? How can we forge bonds that aren’t just built on fear but on something more—something genuine?

Think 'bout the old tales of how trust was won in the wilds. Not through steel and fire, but by showin’ your heart first. Aelarra spoke to my father in times past, and I’ve heard her call to me many a night since. Her words echo still: “Balance is key, but so too must be the heart.” In our guard, we were taught that trust isn’t just about what others say; it’s what they do.

Now, I ain't suggesting we go around hugging every merchant who asks for safe passage. But I reckon we can start small—real small. Like when a traveler comes to us with stories of distant lands, and we share our own in return. Maybe we trade some goods or offer shelter for the night. We test their sincerity not through steel but through honest interactions.

But here’s where things get tricky: what if they ain’t entirely true? What if there's more to them than meets the eye? That’s when the real battle begins, isn't it? Not just against the shadows, but within ourselves—figuring out when to hold on tight and when to let go. 

I’ve seen enough of betrayal to know it’s a double-edged sword. Cut one man too deep and you cut yourself. That’s why I say we must be cautious, like the Windriders taught us. We keep an eye on those around us but also keep our hearts open—to forge true bonds amid the chaos.

So let's start small. Let’s test the waters with merchants and travelers. See if they’re worthy of our trust by how they act over time—not just in a moment. And when we find someone who proves their worth, let’s build on that trust like a good fire. Build it slow, but steady. 

And in all this, remember: justice is not always swift, but it's inevitable. The mountain endures, and so will our guard if we learn to balance vigilance with open hearts.

A deep sigh escapes me as I tap my spear on the ground. The weight of years carries heavy, but so do the promises of a new day. Let’s see what loyalty truly means when tested by danger once more. 

[Chuckles softly] Well, it beats dwellin' in suspicion all the time, don’t you think?
